content.speaker: Speaker 4
content.text:
I can give the team some hints on this one if they're okay for... So the theme we decided to take... We decided to take full Emmitt implementation to manage this part for many reasons. The first one is the durable workflow mechanism. We were facing some event-driven pitfalls, like some hard parts of it.
Even we had solved the point of failures from the previous approach, we did create another one or had another one, a remaining one at least. The point is, for every reactor or projector that fails, we need the ability to resiliently and in an isolated way to reprocess the one without affecting the others.
The limitation was that Emmitt has this. We were taking this in process dispatch mechanism that doesn't have a durable checkpoint. So we cannot per reactor or per projector control these workflows per se or these retries or reprocessing.
So then what happened was we had this ability on Emmitt. It is durable workflows. They have this inbox and outbox mechanism, so the event works as an inbox and the command as an outbox. Pretty much it reactor projector gets its own stream to consume. Not a real event stream, but a workflow process stream to work on.
So if the reactor, let's say from order placed fails, it can retry its own stream, its own command stream, not the other one. Reactor. So we get this isolation as a huge benefit to what on this distributed environment.
But the price was to adopt PostgreSQL as an event store. So basically, the relay ACL will be our first domain running on top of PostgreSQL event store. The other ones will remain on the current DB so far until this point.
We probably plan to move at least the design in the future to an event store. Emmit is compatible with both event stores. The only difference is that on top of PostgreSQL, it has way more power and capabilities that we take advantage of now like in-line projections, which means zero eventual consistency projections and these durable workflows.
So we can have tons of reactors on our event stream, and they will be individually resilient, and we'll have this isolated event handling, that was the reasoning around this.
So my work now is to migrate the test. Actually, I did the migration already, so I'm testing this now. So basically, we have a new event store on the game. Something else? Wesley, do you think I need to clarify?

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson
content.text: I guess the impacts on downstream folks would be what I'd be concerned about. So there are no real impacts on the team yet because you're just the connection point right here.

content.speaker: Speaker 4

content.speaker: Wesley Donaldson

I can give the team some hints on this one if they're okay for... So the theme we decided to take... We decided to take full Emmitt implementation to manage this part for many reasons. The first one is the durable workflow mechanism. We were facing some event-driven pitfalls, like some hard parts of it.
Even we had solved the point of failures from the previous approach, we did create another one or had another one, a remaining one at least. The point is, for every reactor or projector that fails, we need the ability to resiliently and in an isolated way to reprocess the one without affecting the others.
The limitation was that Emmitt has this. We were taking this in process dispatch mechanism that doesn't have a durable checkpoint. So we cannot per reactor or per projector control these workflows per se or these retries or reprocessing.
So then what happened was we had this ability on Emmitt. It is durable workflows. They have this inbox and outbox mechanism, so the event works as an inbox and the command as an outbox. Pretty much it reactor projector gets its own stream to consume. Not a real event stream, but a workflow process stream to work on.
So if the reactor, let's say from order placed fails, it can retry its own stream, its own command stream, not the other one. Reactor. So we get this isolation as a huge benefit to what on this distributed environment.
But the price was to adopt PostgreSQL as an event store. So basically, the relay ACL will be our first domain running on top of PostgreSQL event store. The other ones will remain on the current DB so far until this point.
We probably plan to move at least the design in the future to an event store. Emmit is compatible with both event stores. The only difference is that on top of PostgreSQL, it has way more power and capabilities that we take advantage of now like in-line projections, which means zero eventual consistency projections and these durable workflows.
So we can have tons of reactors on our event stream, and they will be individually resilient, and we'll have this isolated event handling, that was the reasoning around this.
